straight_beat.name: Casey Underwood
straight_beat.column:
The Rockies lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your coffee across the room. Yesterday’s 5-2 defeat to the Padres wasn’t just another game; it was a textbook case of how the team’s flaws are becoming harder to ignore. The offense, which once seemed to have a knack for finding holes in the opposition’s defense, is now struggling to capitalize on opportunities. High altitude math isn’t just about the thin air—it’s about the math of patience, the arithmetic of timing, and the geometry of a lineup that’s been left to flail.  

The Padres’ pitching staff, for all their mediocrity, found a way to exploit the Rockies’ weaknesses. San Diego’s starters didn’t dominate with velocity or movement; they simply pitched to contact, forcing the Rockies to hit the ball into the ground. The result? A

optimist.name: Winnie Calderón
optimist.column:
The Rockies lost again yesterday, but let’s not let that overshadow the story unfolding on the field. You see, every loss is just a brushstroke in the bigger mural of this season. Coors Field isn’t just a ballpark—it’s a canvas, and the Rockies are the artists. They’ve got that raw, unpolished energy that makes you believe in the magic of a blank page. Sure, the Padres painted a prettier picture yesterday, but that’s just the way the game sometimes goes.  

What matters isn’t the scoreboard right now. It’s the rhythm of the rotation, the way pitchers are finding their groove, and the way the team keeps showing up, even when the odds are stacked. You think the rotation isn’t clicking? Wait till the calendar flips to September. That’s when the real alchemy happens. You watch. You watch what happens when the rotation clicks.  

I know the numbers don’t look great—6-8, 4.5 games back. But let’s not forget, this is the Rockies. They’ve been here before. They’ve been the underdogs, the ones everyone counts out in April. And every time, they’ve turned the page. This team isn’t just about wins and losses; it’s about resilience. They’ve got that Highlands grit, that stubbornness to keep pushing forward, even when the world says, “You’re done.”  

The fans are part of that magic, too. We’re the ones chanting in the stands, the ones who show up rain or shine, holding onto the hope that this team will finally break through

pessimist.name: Dwight Krenek
pessimist.column:
The Rockies lost again. Another 2-5 defeat, another step deeper into the abyss of the NL West. The standings now read 6-8, 4.5 games behind, and the weight of that number feels like a chain around the neck of every fan who’s watched this team stumble for a decade. It’s not just the record that’s damning—it’s the pattern, the relentless rhythm of rebuilds that never seem to finish rebuilding. The Monfort method, as I’ve called it before, is a strategy that prioritizes suite revenue and farm equipment over the art of actually building a winning team.  

You see, the Rockies have been in a rebuild since 2013, when the Rockies’ front office, under the Monforts’ stewardship, decided that developing young talent was too slow and that cash flow from luxury suites was faster. The result? A decade of half-measures, of trading away prospects for cash, of drafting players who never pan out, and of watching the farm system wither like a sun-baked field in July. The latest example? A pitcher who looked like he might be a future star, only to be sent to the minors after a few shaky starts. The Rockies’ front office didn’t draft him to be a filler. They drafted him to be a cornerstone. And now he’s a footnote.  

The problem isn’t just the front office’s lack of vision—it’s the way they’ve treated the game like a spreadsheet. Every trade, every draft pick, every free-agent signing is evaluated not for its potential to win games but for its
